entries.title: Air by Christian Kracht review – a metaphysical jigsaw puzzle
entries.summary: A portal opens from our modern world of data centres and interior design into a medieval fantasy realm, in this playfully surreal tale from the author of EurotrashA reclusive interior designer named Paul travels from his home in Orkney to the Norwegian coastal city of Stavanger for a most unusual commission. A data­centre housing a vast internet server is located by a fjord on the outskirts of the
entries.content:
A reclusive interior designer named Paul travels from his home in Orkney to the Norwegian coastal city of Stavanger for a most unusual commission. A data­centre housing a vast internet server is located by a fjord on the outskirts of the city, and a prestigious arts magazine is paying him a large sum of money to give it a makeover. They want him to paint the interiors white – “not just any white, but the perfect white, the singular white”. Shortly after he arrives, a freak weather event causes a power outage, and Paul goes missing; meanwhile the magazine’s jaded editor, Cohen, takes an overdose of barbiturates.

In alternate chapters a very different tale unfolds, set in a distant medieval world redolent of Norse mythology. A man, referred to only as “the stranger”, is nursed back to health by a nine-year-old girl who has shot him with an arrow after mistaking him for a deer. He is being pursued by a vengeful duke, and persuades the girl to join him on the run. Together they leave their plague-ridden town, traverse a barren wasteland and discover a subterranean stone city whose inhabitants live a communal existence, subsisting on seafood and kelp.

The reader experiences Christian Kracht’s new novel, Air, in a state of absorbed bewilderment: how on earth will these two narratives come together? There are obscure connections here and there – in a painting of Merlin and Lancelot that hangs in Paul’s home, and in passing references to neopaganism and Arthurian legend – and one startling anachronism, when the stranger shoots several of the duke’s henchmen with a modern handgun. After Paul’s disappearance, Cohen snoops through his bag and finds an instruction manual for 3D-printing a ceramic pistol. The mechanics of it all are tantalisingly opaque, but it seems as though the outage at the datacentre has produced a glitch that has jumbled up time, space and consciousness, opening a portal between a modern subjectivity and a medieval fantasy world.

During his first meeting with Paul, Cohen speculates that if a person’s thoughts can reside in physical space, as in a digital server, they might eventually transcend that person’s organic life. “What if memory could just persist? Would an entity in data storage have an ego, a self, or not?” Later on, the stranger becomes eerily conscious of himself as a character; he experiences an uncanny sense of deja vu, and has “the definite impression that the world was flattening, in a harmonious way, as though everything were becoming two-dimensional”.

This metaphysical jigsaw puzzle is the ethereal expression of a thoroughly mundane middle-aged ennui. Paul laments “this disgraceful world, with its lack of boundaries, Instagram, and the horrors of vernacular architecture”. He dreams of buying a property on a remote Inner Hebridean island populated only by feral horses. “No electricity, no heating, no phone reception. It was the perfect house.” Cohen is fed up with the modish aesthetics of Nordic chic (“cottagecore lichen restaurants, desaturated colours, that lonely European periphery”), and believes “the wars of the future would be waged out of boredom”.

Swiss author Kracht, who is in his late 50s, has built a reputation as a satirist of gen X affluence. The playful spirit that animated his previous novel, the International Booker-longlisted Eurotrash – in which a wealthy mother and son embark on a sentimental road trip through the Alps – gives way here to a dull, world-weary sardonicism. Paul and Cohen are archetypes of cosmopolitan privilege, their bland self-hatred conveyed in quietly damning cultural references – to Muji stationery, or Paul’s “90s normcore” wardrobe – and an emotionally inert prose, unfussily rendered in Daniel Bowles’s translation from the German. When Cohen overdoses, it is “not out of sadness … but because he just didn’t care”.

Amid such spiritual malaise, there is a bracing, wishful escapism in the sublime visions of natural beauty that punctuate the fantasy narrative: immense ice sheets and rock formations, migrating birds, the aurora. Cohen’s desire to redecorate the data­centre likewise has the feel of a therapeutic intervention: “Hopes, ideas, and memories amassed in such large quantities … painting this space white would ensure order, and clarity …” It’s a slightly ludicrous notion, but also just the kind of thing a dangerously overwrought mind might come up with – an attempt to declutter the psyche through a visual cleanse; a grandiose exercise in self-help.

The narrative artifice is somewhat flimsy, and the exposition oddly perfunctory at times. Yet the novel’s rough-hewn quality befits its evocation of a fuzzy, porous reality. Air is perhaps best understood as a parable – a warning, as it were, from the present. If consciousness, constituted in material infrastructure and therefore intrinsically vulnerable, can be corrupted or distorted like a radio signal, then the spectre of madness might never be too far away. Kracht has taken a familiar premise – brooding creative types, bored of their own cleverness – and made it suggestively strange.

entries.title: Children’s literature festival criticised after requests that comments on diversity be deleted
entries.summary: John McLay, one of the founders of the festival in Bath, contacted writers who had noted a lack of diversity in the programme to request they remove comments posted to social mediaBath children’s literature festival is facing criticism after its founder emailed authors asking them to delete social media posts about the festival’s lack of diversity in its 2026 programme.Authors said that their comm
entries.content:
Bath children’s literature festival is facing criticism after its founder emailed authors asking them to delete social media posts about the festival’s lack of diversity in its 2026 programme.

Authors said that their comments criticising the festival for its lack of representation under a post on its Instagram page were deleted, with the original post later removed and comments limited on other posts.

Several authors said they received emails from one of the founders, John McLay, on Monday evening asking them to delete their comments because they hadn’t “investigated the full line-up before hitting the keyboard”.

John McLay, a children’s literary scout, founded the festival 19 years ago alongside his wife Gill, an agent at Bath Literary Agency.

Gill McLay defended the decision to contact authors directly, saying the festival wanted a post with incorrect information, which claimed there were no writers of colour in the programme, to be removed.

The email cited seven authors of colour in the programme, one of whom is UK-Cypriot, as evidence that the complaints were inaccurate. The programme features almost 100 events in total, and bills itself as the UK’s largest children’s literature festival.

The festival also contacted one author’s agent to ask them to retract their comments.

“Maybe it wasn’t the right way to respond to it, and we have since apologised for that,” said Gill McLay. “It certainly wasn’t our intent to silence anybody at all. It was purely to just try and ensure that it was the right information.”

Children’s author Sophie Anderson, who received the email, told the Guardian: “To have someone so powerful in the industry sending these quite threatening and bullying emails is just horrible.”

Alexandra Sheppard, an author whose video about the situation has amassed more than 20,000 views, said that “the fear of being blacklisted for critiquing powerful organisations feels very real”. She is now organising an open letter to be sent to the festival.

In an Instagram statement, the festival said it is “very proud of this year’s line-up”, but had “listened to the comments made”. While it “chose authors and illustrators from the selection put forward by publishers”, it said it will “work harder to ensure we have greater representation”, adding that it has “already brought new people into the management of the festival to help us with this”.

Authors argue the issue reflects a broader pattern, with similar concerns raised about other events including the London children’s literature festival.

“In 2026, we need to do better,” said author Iqbal Hussain. “We are not living in Regency Bath, nor 1950s London.”

The row comes amid wider concerns about representation in children’s books. Recent Department for Education figures show that 38.7% of school-age pupils in England are from minority ethnic backgrounds, but the eighth Reflecting Realities survey, run by the Centre for Literacy in Primary Education, found that only 24% of books had main characters from racial minorities, down from 30% in 2022.

“I’ve seen countless conversations, panels, articles and social media posts about how publishing can better reflect the young readers it serves,” said Sheppard. “If an organisation like BCLF still lacks meaningful inclusion, the reason is simple: they don’t want it.”

Campaigners warn that limited representation can further alienate young readers who struggle to see their lives reflected in books.

Gill McLay told the Guardian that the festival is working to improve, citing steps such as introducing a steering committee. “All we’re asking of people today is that they give us the opportunity to do it better,” she added.

entries.title: Salman Rushdie attacker faces US terrorism trial
entries.summary: Hadi Matar, already serving 25 years for attempted murder, denies federal charges that could bring a life sentenceThe man who stabbed Salman Rushdie in the eye on stage in 2022 is standing trial for a second time – this time facing terrorism charges after being found guilty of attempted murder and sentenced to 25 years in prison last year.Hadi Matar’s federal trial begins in Buffalo, New York on W
entries.content:
The man who stabbed Salman Rushdie in the eye on stage in 2022 is standing trial for a second time – this time facing terrorism charges after being found guilty of attempted murder and sentenced to 25 years in prison last year.

Hadi Matar’s federal trial begins in Buffalo, New York on Wednesday, about 70 miles from the venue where Rushdie was due to speak about writers in political exile in August 2022, when he was stabbed more than a dozen times.

Matar, 28, was convicted last May of attempted murder and assault at the state level. He denies charges that include providing material support to terrorists and committing an act of transnational terrorism, and faces a possible life sentence if convicted on all counts.

Rushdie, 79, survived the attack but lost the use of one eye and suffered lasting nerve damage. The Booker prize-winning author is expected to testify again, having already given evidence at Matar’s state trial. The trial is expected to last approximately two weeks.

Central to the case is whether Matar was acting on the former ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini’s 1989 fatwa against Rushdie, calling for his death over his novel The Satanic Verses, which some Muslims regarded as blasphemous. Prosecutors have also argued that Matar was acting in service of Hezbollah, the Iran-backed militia in Lebanon.

Matar, a US-Lebanese dual citizen born in California, is reported by relatives to have grown withdrawn after visiting his father in Lebanon in 2018.

Investigators say Matar’s electronic devices and a personal journal showed detailed research into how an attack might be carried out. They also say it included a note weighing the case for acting swiftly given Rushdie’s age – that he could die “any day now”, “So NOW or NEVER!!!”. Photographs of Khomeini and his successor, Ali Khamenei, were reportedly found in Matar’s New Jersey bedroom.

Prosecutors also allege Matar produced and circulated propaganda videos in the months before the attack, incorporating a 2006 speech by the then leader of Hezbollah, Hassan Nasrallah, condemning cartoon depictions of the prophet. Court filings further claim Matar corresponded with an Iran-based contact in the months before the stabbing who said he knew “where to find Rushdie”. Matar also asked him whether the fatwa remained active.

Rushdie was born in India and moved to Britain aged 14. He won the Booker prize in 1981 for Midnight’s Children, before The Satanic Verses made him the target of threats. Iran has denied involvement in the 2022 attack, though the fatwa itself was never formally withdrawn. In 2024, Rushdie published Knife: Meditations After an Attempted Murder, a memoir about the attack.

entries.title: On Thin Ice by Charlie Walker review – a Siberian adventure goes off the rails
entries.summary: Moments of beauty and horror punctuate this account of an ill-timed sub-zero camping trip In late February 2022, Charlie Walker set off on a hiking holiday in northern Siberia. In temperatures as low as -50C, he intended to trek a thousand miles north along the frozen surface of the river Lena, then out across the solid Laptev Sea to the port town of Tiksi, before finally flying home to London. “T
entries.content:
In late February 2022, Charlie Walker set off on a hiking holiday in northern Siberia. In temperatures as low as -50C, he intended to trek a thousand miles north along the frozen surface of the river Lena, then out across the solid Laptev Sea to the port town of Tiksi, before finally flying home to London. “The plan,” he writes, “was simple in concept.” A sub-zero camping trip through a region populated by wolves and bears and scarred by centuries of historical trauma, in a country that was about to invade one of its neighbours: what could possibly go wrong?

On Thin Ice is Walker’s third travelogue. Previous excursions have included a 43,000-mile bicycle ride across three continents, a 1,000-mile solo walk through the Gobi desert, and a river trip down the Congo in a leaky canoe. When not performing incredible feats of forward motion, according to his website, he gives talks to business leaders on subjects such as “risk taking” and “the line between courage and recklessness”. Reading his latest, it’s clear he’s familiar with the terrain on both sides of that line.

Landing in the Siberian city of Yakutsk with his skis, sled, tent and a “comically huge sleeping bag” Walker is busy provisioning when the full-scale invasion of Ukraine begins. With the Foreign Office advising against travel to Russia, he briefly considers abandoning his plans. But, reasoning that “Yakutsk is comfortably closer to the Alaskan city of Anchorage than it is to Kyiv”, he presses on.

“The journey would be different now,” he writes. “As one of the last Western tourists in Russia, I was presented with a unique opportunity … to record the thoughts and feelings of ordinary Russian people, neither soldiers nor politicians. Living far from the front lines, their various opinions would provide insight into the silent majority of 144 million citizens. This seemed worth the risk of remaining inside a police state where already-limited freedoms of expression could evaporate overnight.”

Armed with a flare gun, a can of pepper spray and a dagger for protection from the wolves and bears, Walker spends weeks trudging along ice roads – zimniki – by day, shivering in his tent at night, and occasionally experiencing the extremes of Siberian hospitality. The lorry drivers he meets on the zimniki stop for photos, sharing coffee and conversation. One scolds him for pitching his tent on the road: many truckers fuel themselves with vodka, while some watch television as they drive. He’s treated to local delicacies: grilled horse ribs, reindeer shin pie, a sausage made from aged horse blood (“the puck of boiled blood escaped its horse-gut housing and smeared across the roof of my mouth, setting fast like some ungodly grouting”). Indigenous Sakha women place a wolf’s penis amulet around his neck and offer pancakes to Bayani, “spirit lord of the taiga”, to safeguard his passage.

Many of the people he encounters are more than willing to talk about Ukraine. “Ukraine’s a corrupt country run by Nazis,” declares one young Sakha man. “Ukraine belongs to Russia,” says another. Even in areas so barren trees struggle to grow, Kremlin propaganda is deeply rooted. “I heard that President Putin is saving the Ukrainian people and I’m happy to know they’re being helped,” a lone Eveni herder tells Walker over a meal of roasted reindeer spine. Almost everyone seems to believe some version of the same story: that Putin saved Russia from the chaos of the 90s, and whatever he does is for the best. One wonders – a million Russian casualties later – how that story has weathered.

More than votive pancakes, more than daggers and flares, it soon becomes apparent that the survival tool Walker really should have packed was the business card of a competent local lawyer. It’s not bears who stalk him across the ice, but the FSB, who take an increasingly hostile interest in this inexplicable Englishman, especially as his intended destination, Tiksi, turns out to be a military base. He is accused of “committing journalism”; of being a spy; people he encounters are harassed, others rush to inform on him. He takes insane risks, recording his own “anti-Putin rants” on a Dictaphone – when anti-war journalists face 15-year sentences – and signing a confession without having it translated first. He ends up in a detention centre, where he spends an agonising month awaiting deportation. Reflecting on his choices, he remembers an old Russian saying: “You saw what you were buying, so eat up, even if you choke on it.”

At the start of his journey, Walker had hoped he might see a rosier side of Siberia. There are moments of beauty – the aurora borealis, rare bursts of warmth from the locals – but almost everywhere else is horror and corruption. Growing sinkholes, decaying towns, roads packed with the skeletons of the prisoners who died building them, a “blackened wasteland” the size of Belgium left by wildfires. Walker evokes it all with a masterly touch. Nobody, after reading this book, will be in a rush to book tickets to Siberia, least of all its author, who is now banned from the country.

entries.title: Venus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell review – art, desire and the rise of the Third Reich
entries.summary: This portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observationsIn the summer of 1937, two exhibitions sponsored by the Nazis opened in Munich. For the Degenerate Art exhibition, hundreds of confiscated works by banned modern artists – including Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, Pablo Picasso – were gathered: a derisive demonstration of the supposedly anti-German spirit of c
entries.content:
In the summer of 1937, two exhibitions sponsored by the Nazis opened in Munich. For the Degenerate Art exhibition, hundreds of confiscated works by banned modern artists – including Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, Pablo Picasso – were gathered: a derisive demonstration of the supposedly anti-German spirit of contemporary culture. The show was also meant as a counterpoint to the first Great German Art Exhibition, essentially an art fair for state-sanctioned work, which ran annually until 1944. The official style of Aryan art was traditionalist neoclassicism; popular subjects included idealised female nudes, pastoral landscapes and heroic battle scenes. Hitler was among the main buyers. You probably haven’t heard of any of those artists.

What if one of them were secretly a Jew, working under a pseudonym? Rebecca Birrell’s debut novel imagines this scenario, telling the story of a fictional Jewish artist who comes of age in Berlin in the last days of the Weimar republic. Hannah Sherman is working as a seamstress for the family business when she learns that she is about to be married off. She runs away, finding lodgings using the “desirable Christian” name of Schmidt, and begins to realise her artistic ambitions. A free-thinking New Woman, she also takes two lovers: Saul, a museum curator, and Charlotte, a dancer at a lesbian bar where Hannah likes to sketch.

We all know where history is going. The worsening economic and political situation – which Hannah, our narrator, prefers to allude to only obliquely – engulfs what might otherwise have been a straightforward story of an artist’s progress. As the Great Depression sets in, Hannah turns to a former client for mending work. Elke Grese, a glamorous older woman, sees Hannah’s art and makes an offer of patronage so generous it would be mad to refuse. But as Hannah soon learns, there are strings attached. How far will she go to survive?

Birrell is an art historian by training; in 2021 she published This Dark Country, an idiosyncratic blend of group biography and cultural criticism about female artists working with still life in early 20th-century England. Her deep understanding of artists and their work is evident here. Both Hannah’s inner thoughts and her conversations with others are filled with imaginative and insightful observations, including but not limited to the old master paintings in Saul’s workplace (the Kaiser Friedrich, a real museum whose holdings were destroyed in a fire in 1945) and the postcard reproductions of famous works that she trades back and forth with family, friends and lovers.

While painting her mother, for instance, Hannah is prompted to reflect on Rembrandt’s series of portraits of an elderly woman believed to be his own mother: “In my favourite, his mother’s ageing skin resembles wax, the wrinkles etched into her flesh echoed in the detail of her embroidered cloak, life weighing so heavily upon her she can hardly keep her eyes open.”

Hannah sees the rest of the world through the eyes of an artist, too. On a car journey: “From the windows of the taxi, the clouds were zinc white, paint fresh from its tube.” This makes her an arresting narrator when it comes to visual description – although perhaps a touch too ready with similes and metaphors: are leaves really “like small hands in the breeze”?

By contrast, her emotional landscape is not as vividly rendered. Without giving away too much of the plot – there are some good twisty turns – it often isn’t clear why Hannah gets involved with certain other characters, who seem more like sketches than people. When talking about feelings, she tends to resort to abstract nouns: “fear stirred in my chest”, “shock gave way to shame” and so on. You get the sense that she doesn’t understand her own motivations. “I didn’t interrogate why I wanted a role,” she admits at one point, when describing her choice to insert herself as a background figure in a painting of someone else. This is not unrealistic: she is, after all, a painter, not a novelist. But it can make for frustrating reading.

Towards the end of the book, Birrell offers a glimpse of her own narratorial range with an abrupt shift in perspective, and for the first time enters the mind of another character. Elke – until now a rather distant and mysterious figure – is sitting in a car on a street in Munich, about to pay a visit to the Great German Art Exhibition of 1942. It’s a welcome moment of strangeness closer to the quirky style of Birrell’s nonfiction: a reminder that literature, like art, is at its best when embracing its fullest capacity for invention.

entries.title: Social Darwinists to tech bros: tracing the long fight against equality in US
entries.summary: Historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s book Country of Lords looks back at the many men who have tried to fight against egalitarianism, from John Adams to Peter ThielThe historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s new book is published amid celebrations of the 250th anniversary of the declaration of independence and its truth “self-evident, that all men are created equal”. Country of Lords, however, is a history of Neo-
entries.content:
The historian Kim Phillips-Fein’s new book is published amid celebrations of the 250th anniversary of the declaration of independence and its truth “self-evident, that all men are created equal”. Country of Lords, however, is a history of Neo-Aristocrats, Social Darwinists, Tech Utopians, and the Long Fight Against Equality in America.

Concise and hard-hitting, the book arrives at a time of glaring social and economic inequality, Phillips-Fein seeing Elon Musk “emerging as a trillionaire very much on people’s minds, with a deep level of discomfort, both fear and anger about what this portends”.

“It’s always hard to know what’s going to be happening when you have written a book,” she said. “But I think those questions about economic equality and the impact of extreme levels of economic hierarchy and concentration, and the questions, ‘Can you still have a democracy any longer? What are the ways that you can counter that? What alternate visions are there if you want to live in a world that’s not characterized that way?’, are present.”

Phillips-Fein studies conservatism and “questions about capitalism and democracy”. Her first book, Invisible Hands, was a study of businessmen who fought the New Deal of the 1930s. Her second, Fear City, about the New York economic crisis of the 1970s, was a finalist for the Pulitzer prize. Hers is a major voice. It’s also unique. Not many books start with founder John Adams and end with Peter Thiel of Palantir, Musk’s fellow techno-fascist. Might such company seem unfair on Adams, the Boston lawyer who was part of a great revolution but feared what was unleashed?

“I hope this comes through,” Phillips-Fein said. “Adams is very different from [steel magnate] Andrew Carnegie and [Yale professor] William Graham Sumner and the late 19th-century social Darwinists, and the pro-slavery advocates. He’s also definitely very different from people who are saying extreme levels of economic inequality are good.” Adams, however, was “very ambivalent and skeptical of egalitarian claims … during and after the French Revolution: a deep sense of you can have too much egalitarianism.

“In his writing, he does also give a strong kind of conservative temperament and foundational sense of the problems of arguing for greater equality and a deep wariness and sense of pessimism about a politics that claims to do that.”

And so Adams takes his place in Country of Lords. Other subjects include preachers of race-based inequality from George Fitzhugh, a Confederate theorist of slavery as natural good, to Theodore Lothrop Stoddard, a hack journalist who had a huge bestseller in 1920 with The Rising Tide of Color Against White World-Supremacy, “part of a larger intellectual subculture of ‘scientific’ racism … in the aftermath of world war one”. There is also Carnegie, the Scottish-born tycoon who flourished in the Gilded Age of the late 19th century.

“What makes Carnegie so interesting is that he at least allows himself to have some sense of ambivalence,” Phillips-Fein said. “He says that while the concentration of wealth is absolutely necessary and inevitable, and the people at the top … should have the ultimate say over resources, he is deeply aware that this is politically dangerous, that … it creates an unstable society in which the poor and the working class might actually hate the wealthy, and that’s not in the interest of the wealthy in the long run. Also, he has a sense that human brotherhood is lost, or some kind of community that had existed.”

Carnegie’s father was a Chartist, seeking working-class rights. Carnegie’s major outlet for doubts about his wealth came through philanthropy, prominently the construction of libraries for working people.

“He had a very deep sense of paternalism,” Phillips-Fein said. “He was very invested in the idea of business as a romance and kind of a space of adventure and conquest and feudal benevolence. I think Carnegie’s answer was ultimately that the wealthy should have their money but they shouldn’t bequeath much to their descendants, they should invest it in massive philanthropic projects, and this was better than raising wages or other things like that … It is his level of guilt and ambivalence that I think does make him appealing, if also troubling. I think it raises a set of questions that he certainly was never really able to resolve.”

The 1892 Carnegie Steel strike and its violent suppression devastated Homestead, Pennsylvania, and left Carnegie disillusioned. Now, in Washington, Carnegie Science is the Qatari embassy. At Mount Vernon, the Carnegie library hosts an Apple Store.

Country of Lords is also a primer on modern inequality. In the early 1900s, Henry Ford mixed mass auto production with vicious racism. In mid-century, William Shockley was a proto-tech mogul “who went completely off the rails”.

“He was a co-inventor of the transistor and he figures in different histories of Silicon Valley because he ran this company and wanted to maintain a tremendous amount of control, and as a result a bunch of the employees quit and founded their own firm [two eventually founding Intel] … So after Shockley Labs fell apart, Shockley started to become drawn to racist science, pseudoscience and IQ testing in particular. I don’t think anybody really knows exactly what triggered this, but he starts to talk a lot about it publicly in the mid-60s. He was at Stanford at the time. He was supposed to be teaching physics and engineering.”

Shockley, Phillips-Fein says, was really “a uniquely horrible person, and probably quite unhappy as well.” If that sounds familiar, also like Musk, Shockley wasn’t unique. Linked to the meritocratic movement of the mid-century, he was “not isolated. He is actually part of what is a growing, troubling community of people who coalesce in the late 60s, early 70s, and who are really responding, not always directly, to the context of the civil rights movement.

“It was obviously focused on overturning racist legislation that undergirded segregation, but it also made claims about the distribution of resources, saying the reasons for Black poverty are not innate, they reflect centuries of legal and political structures that have upheld white wealth and laid the foundation for exploitation of Black workers and working-class people, and the political disfranchisement has resulted in this distribution of resources. In other words, that’s nothing to do with merit at all.”

In such soil, Phillips-Fein finds the roots of “the tech titans of today”, rejecting liberal ideals.

“They are very skeptical and hostile towards elite institutions, or at least, have a very broad relationship to them,” she said. “So Peter Thiel embraces this set of ideas about competition drawn from the economist Joseph Schumpeter. He writes about how companies in a normal marketplace are competing at the margins … but every so often someone will come along and create a new invention, usually that winds up dominating the marketplace and changing it, and whoever does that first will completely change the marketplace.

“Thiel has the same idea. He kind of writes about how, ‘I was growing up, I wanted to be the best, I wanted to go to these elite institutions. I went to law school, I had the straight and narrow path, but then I realized that all that was wrong, and actually you had to disrupt everything, create your own monopoly, a monopoly of one: nobody else can ever be you,’ and then the people who are kind of blessed with this genius to recognize how to create something completely new in the world, those are the people who will deserve to win in this race and deserve to command the resources of society.”

The Country of Lords endures.

  • Country of Lords, published by WW Norton & Company, is

    out now

entries.title: Cool Machine by Colson Whitehead review – final part of the Harlem Trilogy is freewheeling fun
entries.summary: The grit and grandeur of 80s New York drive this rollicking story of an underground wheeler-dealerIn the summer of 1977 a bolt of lightning struck a substation on the Hudson River, knocking out power across New York City. Two nights of darkness sparked a wave of looting as Harlem and Bronx teenagers swiped high-end items from the downtown stores. They took speaker systems and turntables to experim
entries.content:
In the summer of 1977 a bolt of lightning struck a substation on the Hudson River, knocking out power across New York City. Two nights of darkness sparked a wave of looting as Harlem and Bronx teenagers swiped high-end items from the downtown stores. They took speaker systems and turntables to experiment with at home. They taught themselves how to scratch and mix, and sang self-styled scat lyrics into stolen microphones. Hip-hop, according to urban legend, was the child of a lightning strike and a robbing spree.

It’s art from crime, thinks Ray Carney, the hardscrabble hero of Colson Whitehead’s rollicking Cool Machine, the final part of his Harlem Trilogy. Or possibly vice versa, given that the man dislikes rap music and nurses a sneaking affection for old-school smash-and-grab. For years Carney has led the sort of intrepid double life that would make Spider-Man proud, supplementing his furniture business on 125th Street with a lucrative sideline as an underworld fence. In his stealthy, dogged fashion, he helps facilitate a process he describes as “the churn”: the movement of stolen goods through New York. The churn fuels the economy and helps refresh the culture. It’s the city’s equivalent of an endocrine system.

The Harlem Trilogy, like its hero, is a cool-headed trickster, more ambitious and far-reaching than its dimestore facade would suggest. Carney’s tale can be relished as a set of shaggy-dog crime capers that bound from the high-stakes heist to the mysterious bag of loot to the fraught getaway through the rat-infested back alley. But it is also – perhaps principally – a story of New York through the decades, and its semi-autonomous circles of black and white society. Harlem Shuffle, the first book in the series, hotwired the action to the 60s civil rights movement. The second, 2023’s Crook Manifesto, played out during the 70s “Fear City” era, when the infrastructure collapsed, the murder rate spiked and Carney’s store doubled as a blaxploitation film set.

Now along comes Cool Machine, the grandstanding final chapter, which pitches us into the dirty, regenerative 1980s. That means hip-hop on the stereo, graffiti on the subway cars and a widening gulf between rich and poor. It means, in this case, a plot that plays like pop-art Marxist noir, jam-packed with cultural references and thrumming with class and racial tensions.

Searching for a secret route into the five-star Waldorf Astoria, Carney encounters a Morlock-style community in the train sheds beneath Manhattan, a subterranean tent city governed by the self-styled “Mayor Crotch”. The homeless people in the tunnels, Carney realises, are casualties of the prosperity above. “The richer those up top got, the more miserable and numerous those below became. And the more cast-offs and have-nots, the higher you have to build to get away from them.”

Carney is a creature of the underworld too, but he’s also a middleman or a bridge, directing the traffic up and down to the point where the colour line becomes porous. Skipping through three interlinked tales from 1981 to 1986, Cool Machine uncovers the seeds of 21st-century cultural appropriation in the trade of African artefacts and sports memorabilia. White and black crooks squabble over the ownership of a Jesse Owens Olympic medal. A Ngil mask from Gabon electrifies the grungy East Village arts scene. A decade ago, Carney’s director friend Zippo was shooting blaxploitation pictures uptown. Now he’s hanging with the arthouse crowd, working on a redubbed version of Alfred Hitchcock’s 1963 classic in which the N-word is substituted for every mention of birds. Hitchcock’s original film was always a parable for racial desegregation, Zippo insists. So he’s simply taking the subtext and making it text.

Whitehead initially envisioned the Harlem Trilogy as a jaunty entertainment, a break from the serious Pulitzer-winning business of The Underground Railroad and The Nickel Boys. Cool Machine specifically covers the New York of his youth, a scene he experienced first-hand, which only adds to the air of vivid, freewheeling fun. Whitehead maps the streets, labels the landmarks and dips in at intervals with handy background information. He takes a dark and dangerous corner of history and makes it feel illuminated, magnified, even borderline family-friendly.

It’s the churn, paradoxically, that keeps the tale honest. It adds grit and grandeur to the sugar hit of nostalgia. The churn, Carney decides, applies to more than the flow of stolen goods. It is the great whip and roil of the city itself; the successive waves of immigrants washing through the individual neighbourhoods. “The enclaves and ghettoes rose and fell and were reborn. That was the churn, too – the underlying melody of death and rebirth in blocks and avenues. You wake up to discover the city has unveiled another version when you weren’t looking.” Carney is now in his 50s and has one eye on the exit door. He’s toiling to maintain his position, fighting the currents of a city that is as ageless and elemental as the Mississippi River. New York, he comes to realise, doesn’t care whether he lives or dies. It’s all-seeing and indifferent and just keeps rolling along.
